coredata.dc:title: Dual-use of sodium alginate to prepare sodium algenate -derived carbon dots sodium algenate hydrogel composite for Pb2+ removal
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Yuxin
Li, Yu
Wang, Guixin
Zhang, Meiling
Peng, Xiongxin
Yang, Fan
Cheng, Kui
coredata.dc:description: The effective mitigation of the environmental impact of heavy metal remains a significant scientific challenge. In this study, a novel and environmentally friendly approach has been proposed to fabricate three-dimensional (3D) functional composite materials for Pb(II) removal. The easy-to-recycle spherical adsorbent is prepared by the dual utilization of sodium alginate (SA) as a bio-source precursor for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) and as a natural framework for the formation of SA hydrogel. The abundant surface-active groups on the SA hydrogel not only provide additional adsorption sites for the aggregation of heavy metal ions but can also be readily cross-linked with CDs without chemical additives, and thereby offering a 3D porous structure for chelating metal ions. The resulting HL-5CDs adsorbent demonstrates an impressive adsorption capacity of 305.21 mg·g−1 for Pb(II). The adsorption mechanism involves both chemical reaction and diffusion, leveraging the porous structure and rich oxygen groups of the material. Notably, a pot experiment revealed that the as-prepared HL-5CDs could inhibit the bioavailability of Pb(II). This study introduces a green and straightforward method for creating high-performance adsorbents, showcasing potential applications in energy, cleaning, and the remediation of wastewater pollution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adsorption
Carbon dots
Sodium alginate
3D hydrogel
Pb2+ removal

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this research by the National Nature Science Foundation of China , the Outstanding Youth Foundation of Heilongjiang Province, the Postdoctoral Scientific Research Developmental Fund of Heilongjiang Province (LBH-Q19067) and the Scientific Research Foundation for Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars of Heilongjiang Province.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation process of HL-CDs composite. (b) The optical photograph of HL-CDs (the CDs concentrations are 0, 0.1, 0.5, 2.5, 5 and 10 g·L−1 from left to right). (c) The XPS spectra of CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s. (d) and O1s. (e) of CDs and SA. (f) of CDs. XRD pattern. (g) and FTIR spectroscopy. (h) TEM image of CDs, the inset is HRTEM image. (i) DLS size distribution by number (%) of CDs. (j) The PL spectra of CDs before and after adsorption.

Effects of different content of CDs (a), different ion competition (0.5 mmol) (b), and solution pH (c) on Pb(II) removal performance of HL-CDs samples. (d) The Zeta potential of CDs. (e) Kinetic curve of Pb(II) adsorption by HL-5CDs. (f) Intraparticle diffusion model. Isotherm of Pb(II) adsorption by HL-5CDs at different temperature of 298 K (g), 308 K (h), and 318 K(i). The initial Pb(II) concentrations is 100 mg·g−1 and the amount of adsorbent was 0.25 g·L−1.

FTIR patterns (a), XPS survey spectrum (b), High-resolution XPS spectra of Pb 4f (c), C 1s (d) and O 1s (e) of HL-5CDs before and after adsorption. The SEM-EDS (f) and STEM-EDS (g) elemental mapping of HL-5CDs after adsorption. (h) Schematic diagram of possible Pb(II) removal mechanism by HL-5CDs.

(a) Digital photographs of epipremnum aureum cultured in tap water containing Pb (10 mg·L−1). Height, leaf length, leaf width and root length (b), Pb content (c), wet weight and dry weight (d), SOD and CAT activities (e) in the epipremnum aureum under different treatment after 20 days.
The comparison of Pb2+ adsorption capacity with other reported biomass adsorbents.
Thermodynamic parameters for the adsorption of HL-5CDs.

head.ce:title: Dual-use of sodium alginate to prepare sodium algenate -derived carbon dots sodium algenate hydrogel composite for Pb2+ removal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Water Conservancy and Civil Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Water Conservancy and Civil Engineering, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150030, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Engineering, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effective mitigation of the environmental impact of heavy metal remains a significant scientific challenge. In this study, a novel and environmentally friendly approach has been proposed to fabricate three-dimensional (3D) functional composite materials for Pb(II) removal. The easy-to-recycle spherical adsorbent is prepared by the dual utilization of sodium alginate (SA) as a bio-source precursor for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) and as a natural framework for the formation of SA hydrogel. The abundant surface-active groups on the SA hydrogel not only provide additional adsorption sites for the aggregation of heavy metal ions but can also be readily cross-linked with CDs without chemical additives, and thereby offering a 3D porous structure for chelating metal ions. The resulting HL-5CDs adsorbent demonstrates an impressive adsorption capacity of 305.21 mg·g−1 for Pb(II). The adsorption mechanism involves both chemical reaction and diffusion, leveraging the porous structure and rich oxygen groups of the material. Notably, a pot experiment revealed that the as-prepared HL-5CDs could inhibit the bioavailability of Pb(II). This study introduces a green and straightforward method for creating high-performance adsorbents, showcasing potential applications in energy, cleaning, and the remediation of wastewater pollution.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Lead (Pb) stands out as a highly detrimental metallic pollutants in aquatic ecosystems, characterized by its persistent presence and considerable toxicity. Industrial activities, including painting, electroplating, and battery manufacturing, represent the primary sources of this contaminant [1]. The present of Pb(II) in water bodies leads to their accumulate in organisms and subsequent infiltratin into the human body through the food chain, resulting in adverse health effects, such as kidney damage, liver failure, endocrine system disorders, brain damage, and neurological dysfunction [2]. To date, various conventional techniques have been employed to address Pb(II) removal from wastewater, encompassing reductive precipitation [3], membrane separation [4], photocatalytic reduction [5], ion exchange [6], and adsorption [7]. Among these, adsorption has gained widespread acceptance due to its cost-effectiveness, simplicity, ease of operation, and high efficiency [8]. However, the low adsorption efficiency and the complexities related to the recovery and reuse of adsorbents, remain significant barriers to their widespread application. In particular, while certain adsorbents exhibit high efficacy in removing heavy metal ions, their inherent instability during preparation and/or adsorption process can introduce new environmental risks. Therefore, the key to purging contaminants from polluted water systems lies in exploring and utilizing adsorbents with elevated adsorption capacity, economical sourcing, selectivity, and environmental friendliness [9].

Carbon dots (CDs) represent a new generation of carbon nanomaterials [10], characterized by straightforward synthesis methods and the utilization of a wide range of raw materials, making them a prominent focus of research in recent years [11]. Compared to other nano-particles, CDs demonstrate high water solubility, tunable photoluminescence, outstanding optical properties, and remarkable photostability[12 13]. These unique characteristics position CDs for a multitude of potential applications, including sensing [14], biomedicine [15], energy storage [16], catalysis [17], anticounterfeiting [18], and optoelectronic devices [19]. With their biodegradable, non-toxic, renewable, and the presence of numerous carboxyl and hydroxyl groups facilitating electrostatically interacting with heavy metals, CDs hold significant promise for the removal of heavy metals from water [20]. Despite extensive exploration in the field of optics, limited research has been directed toward assessing the adsorption performance of CDs in wastewater treatment [21].

Regarding adsorption, the remarkable water solubility of CDs introduces challenges in their separation, leading to the development of CDs composite materials. For instance, Hu et al. [22] synthesized a novel type of polyester carbon dots (TCDs) through a solvothermal method and modified them onto chitosan non-woven fabric (CNF) for the efficient detection and adsorption of Cr(VI). Similarly, Helinando et al. [23] produced a novel variety of fluorescent carbon dots using hydrothermal treatment of amino acids, incorporating them into electrostatic spinning for efficient detection and adsorption of cationic dyes (methylene blue and crystalline violet). Wang et al. [24] manufactured magnetic polyethyleneimine-functionalized CDs/MnFe2O4 nanocomposites, confirming the formation of nanohybrid composites and their integration of fluorescence properties with magnetism separation. However, the applications of CDs are constrained by the non-renewable nature or intricate procedures involved in the synthesis of their precursors, presenting a significant impediment to their widespread application [25]. Consequently, the exploration of bio-sources as green precursors for CDs synthesis emerges as a promising avenue, offering the potential to create cost-effective materials for future applications in heavy metal remediation [26].

Sodium alginate (SA) is a by-product of iodine and mannitol extracted from kelp or sargassum of brown algae, which has been frequently employed in medical, food, and environmental fields [27]. Comprising β-D-mannuronic acid and α-L-guluronic acid, it forms a linear polymer through connections made by 1–4 glycosidic bonds [28]. This unique molecular composition allows them to cross-link with each other to form hydrogels. Hydrogels are three-dimensional lattices of hydrophilic polymers immersed in a substantial volume of water molecules, exhibiting dual characteristics of both solids and liquids. They possess the unique ability to swell without dissolution [29]. The abundant functional groups on the hydrogel can be cross-linked with heavy metal through covalent or non-covalent bonds. Previous reports have outlined the adsorption characteristics of SA hydrogen. Additionally, several reports [30] have demonstrated SA can be used as a raw material for synthesizing CDs. Therefore, in this study, the combination of CDs and hydrogel to form 3D functional composite materials by dual-use of the SA as bio-sources precursors are prepared for heavy metal remediation. CDs are initially synthesized through a thermal solvent method using SA as precursors. Subsequently, these CDs were cross-linked with calcium alginate, resulting in the fabrication of CDs hydrogel spheres through a two-step process. The investigationdelves into the fundamental properties of CDs and their adsorption capacity for Pb(II) in CDs hydrogels spheres (HL-CDs). Optimal adsorption parameters are identified and the influence of functional groups on Pb(II) adsorption is thoroughly examined. Furthermore, practical applications of CDs are evaluated through competitive ion simulations, pH optimization, and plant adsorption experiments. The adsorption mechanisms were probed using various characterization techniques, alongside kinetic and thermodynamic model calculations. In essence, this study presents a synthetic process that harnesses the adsorption capabilities of CDs to create an environmentally friendly adsorbent characterized by low cost, ease of recycling, environmental friendliness, and considerable application potential. Moreover, it scrutinizes the adsorption capacity and mechanisms of CDs spheres, laying a solid foundation for their future practical implementation as adsorbent materials.
Sodium alginate and anhydrous calcium chloride were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All analytical reagents were utilized without further purification.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using a K-Alpha instrument from Thermo Scientific (USA) to analyze elemental and chemical states of the samples. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed using Nicolet iS50 instrument from Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA). The surface morphology and microstructures of the as-prepared materials were observed through field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-6480A, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI TECNAI G2, USA). The Zeta potential of the as-prepared materials was determined using a Zeta potentiometer (Zeta PALS Brookhaven). The concentration of Pb(II) remaining in solution was determined by Inductive Coupled Plasma Emission Spectrometer (ICP). The dynamic light scattering (DLS) was measured by Particle Size and zeta potential Analyzer (Malvern, Britain).

CDs were synthesized using a straightforward hydrothermal synthesis method [31]. Specifically, 2 g of sodium alginate was thoroughly mixed with 100 mL ultrapure water and the mixture was transferred to a 200 mL hydrothermal reaction kettle and then heated to 180 °C for 10 h. Following the reaction, the product was placed in a dialysis bag for separation and purification. During the dialysis process, ultra-pure water was employed for dialysis every 10 h for approximately two days to obtain the final CDs product.

A total of 0.4 g of SA and different amounts of CDs (2, 10, 50, 100 and 200 mg) were combined with 20 mL deionized water under magnetic stirred vigorously for 6 h to form a homogeneous mixture, and then the resulting solution was slowly dropped into 60 mL 0.1 mol·L−1 CaCl2. The obtained CDs hydrogels were soaked in the CaCl2 solution for 12 h to ensure complete gelation. The product, denoted as HL-xCDs (with x representing the concentration gradient, i.e., 1, 2, 3, etc). was then vacuum-sealed for storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Batch adsorption experiment, kinetic, and isotherm
ce:section.ce:para:
The adsorption capacity was calculated according to Eq. (1) [32].
(1)

V (
C 1

C 2
)
m

Where q e (mg·g−1) is equilibrium absorption capacity, V (L) is solution volume, C 1 (mg·L−1) denotes initial concentration, C 2 (mg·L−1) refers to the concentration of the remaining solution, and m (g) represents the number of HL-CDs.

In the kinetic experiment, HL-CDs was dosed at 0.25 ± 0.01 g·L−1, and the initial Pb(II) concentration was 100 mg·L−1. The q e of HL-CDs on Pb(II) was measured at 0, 30, 60, 120, 240, 480, 720, 960, 1200, 1440, 1800 and 2160 min. Pseudo-first-order (Eq. (2)), pseudo-second-order kinetics (Eq. (3)), elovich kinetics (Eq. (4)) and W&M kinetics (Eq. (5)) were used to analyze the data [33].

1 / 2

In the isotherm experiment, the dosage of HL-CDs was 0.25 ± 0.01 g·L−1, and the concentration of Pb(II) was 10–400 mg·L−1. The q e of HL-CDs on Pb(II) was analyzed by Langmuir (Eq. (6)), Freundlich (Eq. (7)), Tempkin (Eq. (8)). The meanings of model parameters are provided in the supplementary materials [9].

A Pb(II) aqueous solution (200 mL, 10 mg·L-1, and pH 6.5) was placed in a plastic container. Subsequently, 10 water spinaches (length of 20–25 cm and diameter of 4.0–6.0 cm) were evenly placed in foam with a hole (diameter of 0.8 cm). Subsequently, HL-CDs (50 mg) was placed in Pb(II) aqueous solution of experimental group. After that, the pots were placed under the following conditions: temperature of 25 ℃ and humidity of 60 %. Moreover, the wet weight, dry weight, height, yellow leaf ratio, root length, and Pb content in water spinach were measured after 20 d. All experiments were performed in triplicates.

As depicted in Fig. 1 a, a straightforward, environmentally friendly approach was designed to fabricate a 3D functional composite materials by dual-use of the SA as the bio-sources precursors for the preparation of CDs and as a natural framework for the formation of 3D structures. The plentiful surface-active groups on the SA hydrogel not only offer additional adsorption sites for the aggregation of heavy metal ions but can also be readily cross-linked with CDs without chemical additives, thus providing more adsorption sites for chelating metal ions. As a result, the as-prepared HL-CDs adsorbents exhibit characteristics of biodegradability, along with simple and environmentally friendly preparation methods. Simultaneously, we investigated the impact of varying amounts of CDs on the HL-CDs composite materials. As depicted in Fig. 1b, the addition of CDs did not compromise the structure of the hydrogel but rather intensified its color. Structure analyses through XPS, XRD and FTIR analyses were carried out to gain insights into the composition and characteristics of HL-CDs adsorbents.

As depicted in Fig. 1c, the XPS full spectra of CDs exhibits the characteristic peaks of C1s and O1s. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1 s and O1s were subjected to analyze by Gaussian curve fitting to scrutinize the elemental chemical states. As presented in Fig. 1d, the O 1s XPS spectrum can be filled with three peaks, which are indexed as CO (531.4 eV), CO (535.9 eV) and HOC (533.1 eV). Meanwhile, three peaks of CC/CC/CHx, CO, and COOR centered at 284.8, 286.4 and 288.3 eV were fitted in the C1s peak (Fig. 1e). These results demonstrate that the synthesized CDs possess a significant amount of oxygen-containing functional groups, which prove advantageous for the binding of heavy metal ions. The XRD patterns of SA and CDs within the 2θ range of 5–70° are illustrated in Fig. 1f. In contrast to the typical amorphous structure of SA, the CDs pattern displays distinct features, with the sharp peaks observed at 2θ = 20.83°, 27.68°, 39.48° and 47.57°, which are likely attributed to the (111), (104), (205) and (305) plans of the crystal structure of C (JCPDS card No. 22-1069), consistent with previous studies [34]. The FTIR spectra of SA and CDs are presented in Fig. 1g. A broad band around 3500–3200 cm−1 is observed in the SA spectrum, corresponding to the stretching vibration modes of OH groups. The weak peak at approximately 2938 cm−1 is attributed to the CH antisymmetrical stretching vibration [35]. The peak at about 1623 cm−1 typically represent the carboxylic anion [36] and the peak at about 1387 cm−1 is related to the CO stretching vibration. In comparison with the SA, CDs showed enhanced absorption peaks at about 780 cm−1 and 665 cm−1, indicative of the phenyl group. TEM images of the CDs were acquired to provide detailed insights into their morphology and structure. As depicted in Fig. 1h, the CDs exhibit a spherical and uniform morphology with an average diameter ranging from 3 to 6 nm. The images reveal a high degree of dispersion and minimal agglomeration, indicating a well-controlled synthesis process. The high-resolution TEM images (the insert of Fig. 1h) further unveil the crystalline nature of the CDs, with lattice fringes of 0.23 nm corresponding to specific crystal planes of graphene (100) [37]. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis provides valuable information about the hydrodynamic diameter and polydispersity of nanoparticles. Therefore, DLS was employed to generate the mean size distribution of the as-prepared CDs in solution. The obtained curves, as illustrated in Fig. 1i, indicates a well-defined and relatively uniform size distribution. Consisting with the TEM image, the mean hydrodynamic diameter of CDs is calculated to be 3–6 nm and the polydispersity index of the CDs is 0.1363. These observations corroborate the successful formation of well-defined and monodisperse CDs with promising characteristics. The Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs before and after the adsorption process were analyzed to gain insights into the changes in their optical properties during the interaction with the adsorbate. In the spectra recorded before adsorption, the CDs exhibit characteristic emission peaks indicative of their fluorescence properties. The maximum emission wavelength of CDs is 436 nm, showcasing a relatively high emission intensity in CDs directly prepared from biomass. Subsequent to the adsorption process, a significant quenching of CDs fluorescence in the presence of Pb(II) ions is evident. Alterations in the PL spectra are observed, suggesting potential modifications in the surface of CDs or structure induced by the adsorption of Pb2+. This material has the possibility of Pb ion specific detection in the future.

To investigate the impact of varying CDs content on adsorption, the adsorption properties of HL-CDs composite with different CDs concentrations were compared under identical conditions. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, the inclusion of CDs in the hydrogel matrix significantly enhances the adsorption capabilities of the composite material for Pb(II). With an increase in the quantity of added CDs, the adsorption capacity of Pb(II) by the HL-CDs composite also increases. The maximum adsorption capacity is reached at a CDs concentration of 5 g·L−1. Excessive CDs may induce quantum dot agglomeration, leading to a reduction in overall adsorption capacity. Therefore, the chosen research subject for the subsequent experiments is HL-5CDs. The wastewater comprises numerous coexisting ions alongside Pb(II), potentially engaging in competitive interactions that diminish the adsorption efficiency of Pb(II). In this study, prevalent cations found in wastewater (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+) were employed as competing ions in a simulation to assess their impact on adsorption competition. As shown in Fig. 2b, the competing affinity of cations for Pb(II) at adsorption sites exhibited the sequence Ca2+> Mg2+> Al3+> Na+> K+, which may be attributed to variations in their negative charges and hydration radii. With the exception of divalent cations, the competitive adsorption of Pb(II) by other ions is not pronounced. One of the crucial factors significantly influencing the adsorption process is the initial pH of the metal ions solution. This impact arises from its influence on interactions between the surface species of the adsorbent and the adsorbate ions, as well as the charged species that will develop at the surface of the adsorbent. Fig. 2c presents the adsorption performance of Pb(II) by HL-5CDs concerning pH. It is evident that the removal efficiency of Pb(II) follows a similar trend to the swelling ratio across various pH values. Briefly, based on the pHZPC outcome, the adsorbent was positively charged when the pH was below 2.2, and the competitive effect of H(I) diminished with increasing pH. Optimal removal efficiency occurred at pH 5 where the adsorbent carried a negative charge. A decline in Pb(II) adsorption efficiency at pH 7.0 was attributed to the complexation reaction between Pb(II) and ·OH at elevated pH values. Considering that the pH of the Pb(II) solution in the adsorption experiment was kept within the range of 5.0–6.0, a pH value of 5 was chosen for the adsorption experiment. The zeta potential curve in the pH range of 1.0–7.0 indicates that the pHZPC of CDs was 2.2 (Fig. 2d). If the pH in the solution is below the pHZPC, the surface of CDs becomes positively charged, resulting in the repulsion of cationic heavy metals [38]. When the solution pH beyond 2.2, the adsorbent surface carried negative charges, facilitating the adsorption of Pb(II) ions due to their positive nature [39].

To investigate the time-dependent adsorption of Pb(II) on HL-5CDs, the adsorption of Pb(II) from 0 min to 2160 min was conducted (Fig. 2e). Pseudo-first-order (Eq. (2)), pseudo-second-order kinetics (Eq. (3)), elovich kinetics (Eq. (4)) and W&M kinetics (Eq. (5)) were used to analyze the data. The model parameters are calculated and presented in Table 1 . Obviously, the linear regression of adsorption kinetics was best described by the pseudo-second-order kinetics (R2 = 0.953) (Fig. 2e). This indicates that the adsorption of Pb(II) on HL-5CDs involves a chemical reaction, possibly accompanied by some physical adsorption [40]. To our knowledge, the adsorption capacity depends on the number of adsorption sites. The abundant O groups either in the surface of CDs and as well as the the surface-active groups on the SA hydrogel offer numerous adsorption sites could explaining the excellent Pb(II) removal performance on the as-prepared HL-CDs composite. Additionally, the Elovich model (R2 = 0.987) is employed to fit the adsorption data. The adsorption curves adhere to the intraparticle diffusion model and are categorized into three stages (t < 120 min, 120 min < t < 1440 min, and t > 1440 min) (Fig. 2f). During the stage of t < 120 min, the adsorption rate is remarkably high due to ample initial adsorption sites and the solvent potential difference between the material and the Pb(II) solution. This facilitates the diffusion of Pb(II) ions from the solution to the HL-5CDs, thereby enhancing the adsorption effect [41]. Therefore, in the initial 120 min, the primary diffusion effect is surface diffusion. In the stage of 120 min < t < 1440 min, adsorption diminishes the concentration disparity between the solution and the material. Moreover, some adsorption sites have been occupied, leading to a slowdown in adsorption speed. At this stage, internal diffusion and surface are dominant [42]. When the time beyond 1440 min, the adsorption capacity remained essentially unchanged, indicating that adsorption had reached a dynamic equilibrium. Consequently, the overall adsorption process is jointly governed by intraparticle diffusion and external mass transfer [43].

Adsorption isotherms are employed to analyze the adsorption behavior and assess the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent. In this study, three isotherm models, including Langmuir, Freundlich, and Tempkin were utilized for fitting. The diagram and values of the isotherms are shows in Fig. 2g, h and i and Table 2 . The constant 1/n in the Freundlich equation represents the exchange between interactive sites in the HL-5CDs and the pollutant [16]. The adsorption capability of the adsorbent is characterized by Kf. Table 2 showed that the 1/n value for HL-5CDs was greater than 1, suggesting favorable Pb(II) removal on them. HL-5CDs had b t values higher than 4000 mg·g−1, indicating that oxygen-rich functional groups were more prone to attaching Pb(II). The b t value from Temkin equation can be used to classify the kind of adsorption. In this study, Pb(II) adsorption onto HL-5CDs seemed to be physical as the material had b t values less than 4200 J·mol−1 [44]. The test results affirmed that the isotherms conformed to the Langmuir model, providing evidence that the adsorption of Pb(II) by HL-5CDs is closer to the homogeneous and monolayer adsorption processes. Additionally, as the temperature increases, the adsorption capacity decreases accordingly, consistent with the result in Fig. 2g, h, i, confirming that the increase in temperature inhibits adsorption [45]. The maximum theoretical adsorption capacity, as calculated by the Langmuir isotherm at 298 K, was determined to be 305.2 mg·g−1. This value surpasses the reported capacities of other adsorbents found in the literature (Table 3 ). Moreover, after five cycles (Fig. S1), HL-5CDs can still maintain 69.5 % of the original adsorption capacity.

The viability and characteristics of the Pb(II) removal process were assessed through thermodynamic parameters, revealing a temperature-dependent nature of Pb(II) adsorption. The thermodynamic parameters, including the change in Gibbs free energy (ΔG°, kJ·mol−1), change in enthalpy (ΔH°, kJ·mol−1), and change in entropy (ΔS°, kJ·mol−1), were evaluated as follows:
(9)
Δ
G
0
= Δ
H
0
-
( - T Δ
S
0
)
(10)
Δ
G
0
= -
( - R T l n
K L
)
(11)
ln
K L
=
- Δ
H
0
R
1 T
+
Δ
S
0
R
where T (K) is the Kelvin temperature, R (8.314 J·mol·K−1) is the gas constant, and KL (L·mol−1) is the adsorption equilibrium constant. The related parameters are presented in Table 4 . The ΔG0 values were calculated to be 4.97, 5.34 and 5.72 kJ·mol−1 at 298 K, 308 K and 318 K, respectively. Obviously, the ΔG0 value decreased with the temperature increased, demonstrating that the adsorption mechanism was favorable at low temperatures [59]. The adhesion of Pb(II) to the exterior of the HL-5CDs reduceing the directional disorder, resulted in a negative value of enthalpy ΔH0 (-6.20 kJ·mol−1), suggesting that Pb(II) adsorption on the surface of HL-5CDs was an exothermic reaction. Additionally, the positive value of ΔS0 (37.47 J·mol−1·K−1) indicated increased randomness at the solid–liquid interface during adsorption.

FTIR and XPS characterization of the adsorbent after adsorption is helpful to find out the mechanism of HL-5CDs adsorption of Pb(II). As shown in Fig. 3 a, FTIR spectra revealed significant alterations in peaks located at 3500–3200 cm−1, 1054 cm−1,1608 cm−1, and 818 cm−1, corresponding to OH, ROCO, COOH, and CO after adsorption. This confirms the engagement of four oxygen groups in the adsorption reaction. Notably, the peaks of COOH and CO varied significantly, emphasizing the significance of OCO and CO as the primary reaction sites. Changes in the OH and COOH peaks may suggest their contribution to the reduction of Pb(II). It has been demonstrated that COOH and OH can potentially adsorb Pb(II) through complexation reactions [45]. Except for the representative peaks of C and O appearing in the XPS full survey presented in Fig. 3b, two new photoelectron peaks corresponding to the binding energies of Pb 4f are detected at 139.2 and 144.0 eV after the reaction, indicating that Pb(II) has been attached to the surface of HL-5CDs. Fig. 3c present the characteristic orbitals of Pb 4f spectrum of HL-5CDs after adsorption, the low energy band (139.2 eV for Pb 4f7/2) and the high energy band (144.0 eV for Pb 4f5/2) from the spin–orbit splitting are presented in the curves, which is consistent with data for PbO and Pb(OH)2 [60]. Fig. 3d shows that four peaks at 288.6 eV, 287.5 eV, 286.6 eV and 284.8 eV represented C1s, which corresponded to the non‑oxygenated carbon (CCC, 25.37 %), the carbon in CO (60.18 %), the carbonyl carbon (CO, 1.66 %) and the carboxylate carbon (OCO, 13.63 %), respectively. The peak area of metal- O groups increased from 1.66 % to 3.58 % after Pb2+ adsorption, respectively, indicating the formation of Pb-O. The O 1s spectra were also decomposed with two different peaks (Fig. 4 e): the oxygen that blinds to CO (533.0 eV) and the oxygen in carboxylate or carbonyl (OCO or CO: 531.6 eV), respectively. The peak area ratios of total O groups decreased from 29.92 % to 27.84 % after Pb(II)adsorption, this is due to the strong interactions occurred on oxygen-containing functional groups during the adsorption process. XPS results further supported FTIR findings by demonstrating a significant decrease in the peaks of CO, OCO, and CO in HL-CDs after adsorption. The interaction between oxygen-containing functional groups and metal ions, through complexation reactions, played a pivotal role in the adsorption of Pb(II) onto HL-5CDs, facilitating adsorption/precipitation. The SEM of HL-5CDs after Pb(II) adsorption revealed minimal changes in surface morphology, confirming the successful adsorption of Pb(II) onto HL-5CDs. The SEM-EDS and STEM-EDS elemental mapping analyses indicated a distribution of Pb on the material similar to that of C and O, validating C and O groups as the primary adsorption sites (Fig. 3f and g). Kinetic models suggested that diffusion was the predominant mode of Pb(II) movement on the material. Langmuir models suggested that monolayer chemisorption might be the dominant process for CDs in removing Pb(II). The elucidation of the mechanism underlying the removal of Pb(II) from aqueous solutions by the HL-5CDs complex is depicted in Fig. 3h, drawing upon the aforementioned analysis results. The adsorption performance of HL-5CDs is intricately linked to its physicochemical properties, encompassing functional group activity and pore structure that induce complexation, electrostatic interactions, and ion exchange between heavy metals and the HL-5CDs [61].

To investigate the impact of HL-5CDs on preventing the crops from the adsorption of Pb(II), a pot experiment was performed and epipremnum aureum was chosen as a representative plant for a 20-day experiment. Fig. 4a provides a comparison digital photos of epipremnum aureum before and after the 20-day culture period. The figures show that plants subjected to material adsorption treatment exhibited higher wet weight, dry weight, height, leaf length, leaf width, and root length compared to those without treatment in the presence of Pb(II) in tap water. These results suggest that HL-5CDs can mitigate the negative effects of Pb(II) on water spinach. To further assess the impact of HL-5CDs on the accumulation of Pb(II) in water spinach, the Pb content was measured. As depicted in Fig. 4b and Fig. S2, the Pb content in the roots, stems, and leaves of water spinach with HL-5CDs treatment was significantly lower than that without HL-5CDs treatment. Plant cells possess an antioxidant defense system comprising enzymes like superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), etc., which can reflect the stress resistance of plants. As depicted in Fig. 4e, both the SOD and POD contents in plants with adsorption treatment were lower than those without adsorption treatment, indicating a more favorable living environment for plants with adsorption treatment.

In conclusion, the environmentally friendly synthesis of HL-CDs has proven to be a promising avenue for addressing Pb(II) pollution in wastewater. The innovative combination of SA hydrogel and SA-derived CDs ensures a green and cost-effective process for producing an efficient adsorbent. The comprehensive investigation into the adsorption mechanisms highlights the he crucial role of oxygen-containing functional groups in the adsorption reaction. Furthermore, pot experiments demonstrated that their ability of the as-prepared HL-CDs adsorbent to mitigate the negative effects of Pb contamination on plant growth. The research contributes valuable insights into the adsorption capacity and mechanisms of CDs, paving the way for their potential implementation in water treatment strategies for the removal of heavy metals. Overall, this study opens avenues for the development of sustainable and effective adsorbent materials for environmental remediation.

Yuxin Xu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yu Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Guixin Wang: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Meiling Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Xiongxin Peng: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Fan Yang: Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology. Kui Cheng: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this research by the National Nature Science Foundation of China, the Outstanding Youth Foundation of Heilongjiang Province, the Postdoctoral Scientific Research Developmental Fund of Heilongjiang Province (LBH-Q19067) and the Scientific Research Foundation for Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars of Heilongjiang Province.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
